All who are not slaves of sin and Satan serve God. All gospel truth is in line with being godly, teaching reverence for God. The purpose of the gospel is to raise up hope along with faith; to turn the mind and heart away from the world, and to lift them to heaven and things above. How excellent then is the gospel, which was the subject of Divine promise so early, and what thanks are due for our privileges! Faith comes by hearing, and hearing by the word of God; and whoever is appointed and called must preach the word. Grace is the free favor of God, and acceptance with him. Mercy is the result of that favor, in the pardon of sin, and freedom from all miseries both here and hereafter. And peace is the effect and fruit of mercy. Peace with God through Christ who is our Peace, and with other people and ourselves. Grace is the source of all blessings. Mercy, and peace, and all good come out of this.

The character and qualifications of pastors, here called elders and bishops, agree with what the apostle wrote to Timothy. As such bishops and overseers of the flock, to be examples to them, and God's stewards to take care of the affairs of his household, there is great reason that they should be blameless. What they are not to be is plainly shown, as well as what they are to be, as servants of Christ, and able ministers of the letter and practice of the gospel. And here are described the spirit and practice becoming those who should be examples of good works.

False teachers are described. Faithful ministers must oppose such in good time, that their folly being made clear, they may not go any further. They had a base end in what they did; serving a worldly interest under the appearance of religion: for the love of money is the root of all evil. Such should be resisted, and put to shame, by sound doctrine from the Scriptures. Shameful actions, the reproach of non-Christians, should be far from Christians; falsehood and lying, envious scheming and cruelty, brutal and sensual practices, and idleness and laziness are sins condemned even by the light of nature. But Christian meekness is as far from cowardly overlooking sin and error, as it is from anger and impatience. And though there may be national differences in character, yet the heart of man in every age and place is deceitful and desperately wicked. But the sharpest reproofs must aim at the good of those who are reproved; and soundness in faith is most desirable and necessary. To those who are defiled and unbelieving, nothing is pure; they abuse, and turn things lawful and good into sin. Many profess to know God, yet in their lives deny and reject him. See the miserable state of hypocrites, such as have a form of godliness, but lack power; yet let us not be so quick to fix this charge on others, as careful that it does not apply to ourselves.
